title: 1 Chronicles 3
subtitle: David’s Royal Line Through the Exile

sections.header: Verses 1–9 — David’s Sons Born at Hebron and Jerusalem

refs.note: David’s sons at Hebron listed with the same names but with additional narrative context (Abigail, Maacah, etc.).
The Bathsheba/Uriah affair — completely absent from Chronicles. The Chronicler’s David is the David of worship, not the David of sin.

notes.note: The list of David’s sons reminds us that even the greatest king’s household was marked by complexity and conflict. Polygamy, concubines, and rival sons are the seeds of succession crisis. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: Bathshua (bat-shûaʿ) is likely a variant of Bathsheba (bat-shebaʿ). Ammiel (ʿammîʾēl, “my people is God”) is the reverse of Eliam (ʾĕlîʿam, “my God is people”) — the same consonants rearranged. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

hist.context: David’s sons are listed by mother and birthplace: six born at Hebron during his seven-year reign there, then nine (plus Tamar) born at Jerusalem. The Chronicler omits the circumstances of Solomon’s birth entirely — no Bathsheba, no adultery, no murder of Uriah. “Bathshua daughter of Ammiel” appears as Solomon’s mother (v.5) with no hint of scandal. This is the Chronicler’s most famous omission: David’s greatest sin is erased from the record.

heb.paragraph: The Hebrew mālaḵ (to reign) carries the weight of divinely delegated authority. In the Chronicler's theology, every king reigns as God's representative — their faithfulness or failure directly affects the nation's destiny. The verb appears at the critical transitions that shape Israel's monarchic history.

notes.note: The Chronicler's portrait of David emphasizes his role as the ideal theocratic king — the man after God's own heart who established worship, organized the Levites, and prepared for the temple. David's reign points typologically to Christ, the Son of David, who perfectly fulfills every dimension of the Davidic covenant (2 Sam 7:12-16; Rom 1:3).

The Chronicler's perspective consistently emphasizes God's faithfulness to His covenant promises despite Israel's unfaithfulness. This dual theme — human failure and divine grace — is the heartbeat of all Scripture and finds its ultimate resolution in the cross of Christ, where God's justice and mercy meet perfectly (Rom 3:25-26).

The Chronicler wrote for a post-exilic community needing to understand their identity and hope. His message remains vital: God's promises to David are irrevocable, worship is the center of covenant life, and faithfulness — not circumstances — determines blessing. Believers today can take the same comfort: our God is faithful even when we are not (2 Tim 2:13).

sections.header: Verses 10–24 — Solomon to the Exile and Beyond

refs.note: Jehoiachin released from prison. The genealogy here traces what happened next: Jehoiachin had sons in Babylon, and the line continued.
Zerubbabel son of Shealtiel led the return and rebuilt the altar. He appears here at 3:19 as Jehoiachin’s grandson.
Matthew’s genealogy passes through Jeconiah (Jehoiachin) to Zerubbabel and on to Jesus. The Chronicles genealogy and Matthew’s are tracking the same line.

notes.note: The continuation of David’s line through exile and into restoration teaches that God’s promises outlast every human catastrophe. Thrones may fall, temples may burn, but the covenant seed survives. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The relationship between Zerubbabel and Shealtiel/Pedaiah is textually complex. The Chronicler names Pedaiah as Zerubbabel’s father (v.19), while Ezra and Haggai call him “son of Shealtiel.” Levirate marriage is the usual explanation. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

hist.context: The royal line runs from Solomon through the kings of Judah to the exile (matching the Kings account), then continues beyond — through Jehoiachin’s sons to Zerubbabel and several generations further. This is the most theologically significant section of the genealogies: the Davidic line survives exile. The dynasty that seemed to end at 2 Kings 25 is shown here to continue into the Chronicler’s own time. The promise of 2 Samuel 7 is not extinct.

heb.paragraph: The kissēʾ (throne) in the Chronicler's theology is not merely political furniture but the visible symbol of God's rule through the Davidic line. The promise of an eternal throne (2 Sam 7:13) reverberates through every royal succession. Each king who sits on it is measured against the Davidic standard.

notes.note: Solomon's wisdom was a direct gift from God, granted in response to humble petition rather than selfish ambition (cf. James 1:5). Yet even Solomon's legendary wisdom proved insufficient apart from sustained obedience. The truly wise king whom Solomon foreshadows is Christ, in whom 'are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge' (Col 2:3).

Solomon's reign — in both its glory and its eventual decline — serves as a living parable of the danger of spiritual compromise. The wisest man who ever lived was undone by disobedience to clear commands of Scripture (Deut 17:16-17). If Solomon could fall, no believer dare presume upon their own strength. Sustained faithfulness requires sustained dependence on God's grace.

The judgment of exile was not the end of God's purposes but a severe mercy designed to purify and restore. God disciplines those He loves (Heb 12:6). For the believer, seasons of divine discipline are not evidence of God's abandonment but of His fatherly care. He prunes every branch that bears fruit so that it will bear even more (John 15:2).

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVthe third, Absalom the son of Maakah daughter of Talmai king of Geshur; the fourth, Adonijah the son of Haggith;ESVthe third, Absalom, whose mother was Maacah, the daughter of Talmai, king of Geshur; the fourth, Adonijah, whose mother was Haggith;NIVand these were the children born to him there: Shammua, Shobab, Nathan and Solomon. These four were by Bathsheba daughter of Ammiel.ESVThese were born to him in Jerusalem: Shimea, Shobab, Nathan and Solomon, four by Bath-shua, the daughter of Ammiel;

themes.note: David’s Royal Line Through the Exile

coaching.tip: David's sons are listed in detail, then the line continues through the exile into the post-exilic period. The Chronicler traces the Davidic line to his own day — a quiet argument that the dynasty still exists and the covenant is still active, even without a throne.

prayer_prompt: David's royal line continued through exile and beyond. Consider asking God about the promises He's keeping even when circumstances suggest they've failed.
